0.1.label: Question 0.1
0.1.scenario: PMI is usually a one-year contract. Each year the insurer will offer renewal of the contract with (usually) unchanged terms, but with a new premium. Some policyholders, particularly the older ones, object to these arrangements.

(i).question: Why might some policyholders be unhappy with this?

Why some policyholders are unhappy: The premiums will increase each year in line with the expected claims cost, which will increase as policyholders get older. [1]

There may come a time when, despite the fact that the renewal premium is fair, ie equal to the expected claims cost plus a small loading, the consumer is unable to find the resources to pay the premium. [½]
So, policyholders are faced with the possibility of lapsing the policy and taking on the risk themselves. [½]

(ii).question: Why is it difficult for insurers to offer policies that would remove this issue?

Why it is difficult for the insurer: In order to offer regular level premiums for a long-term contract, the insurer must be able, in a statistical sense, to assess the long-term risks. [½]
This may be hard because the average claim size is unlikely to remain the same over time. [½]
This may be due to changes in:
the kinds of ailments that require treatment [½]

As one ailment becomes treatable in a cheap and effective way, so other ailments become more prevalent and are treated using regimes that have developed in response to the emergence of the new disease. [½]
The costs of these emerging treatment regimes are difficult to predict, so it is difficult for insurers to estimate the long-term expected claims costs. [½]
The distribution of the estimates will have a large variance and result in the requirement for large contingency loadings in the premiums. [½]
The resulting premium levels may be too high to be acceptable. [½]

0.2.label: Question 0.2
0.2.scenario:
A health and care insurance company writes:
(a) critical illness policies secured by level premiums throughout the term of the policy
(b) long-term care insurance policies secured by a single premium paid at the start of the contract.
0.2.question: Describe how new business strain is likely to arise in each case. [3]

New business strain: New business strain occurs when the initial asset share (premiums received less initial expenses) is less than the sum of the supervisory reserves and the solvency capital requirement. [½]
Any such strain has to be made good from the company’s free assets (ie those assets not earmarked for any particular purpose). [½]
(a) Critical illness insurance: For regular premium contracts the asset share is normally negative in the early years. [½]
This is because there is a high level of expenses incurred at the start of the contract, in particular from commission, sales and marketing, underwriting and policy documentation, … [½]
… and these costs are likely to exceed the amount of the first premium received. [½]
The reserves may need to be at least zero because of supervisory restrictions, plus solvency capital may be required too. [½]
Thus the value of the liabilities exceeds the value of the assets, and there is new business strain. [½]
(b) Long-term care insurance: It is common to have new business strain, despite the large positive net cash inflow (the single premium) at time zero. [½]
New business strain often arises due to the valuation basis being stronger than the premium basis … [½]
… and/or due to the requirement to hold solvency capital. [½]
